original_hypothesis.title: Payer Can Unilaterally Cooperative-Close Channel, Stealing Unsettled Payee Earnings
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The close() function is labeled 'cooperative close' but does NOT require the payee's signature. It only requires signatures from authorizedSigner and payer. Since the payer typically controls the authorizedSigner (or IS the authorizedSigner), the payer can unilaterally finalize the channel at any time with cumulativeAmount = channel.settled (or 0), bypassing the grace period entirely. The payee receives nothing for any unsettled service already provided.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This breaks the core payment guarantee of the protocol. The grace period (1 hour) exists to protect the payee during unilateral close, but cooperative close bypasses it entirely without payee consent. A dishonest payer can consume service, accumulate vouchers, and then immediately close the channel before the payee settles.

proof_of_signal.pattern: close() verifies signer==authorizedSigner and payerSigner==payer but never checks payee consent. Payer controls both required signatures.

preconditions: Payer controls the authorizedSigner key (default case per design docs)
Channel has unsettled voucher value (payee provided service but hasn't settled on-chain)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: close() requires only payer + authorizedSigner signatures, not payee signature
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Payer signs voucher with cumulativeAmount=channel.settled, signs CLOSE message, calls close(). Grace period completely bypassed.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Payer recovers deposit - settled immediately, stealing all unsettled service value from payee

economic_questions: What is the typical ratio of settled vs unsettled value in active channels?
Can the payee detect and settle vouchers before the payer's close tx is included (no mempool visibility)?
Is there any off-chain mechanism that prevents this (e.g., service stops without settlement)?
validation_requirements: Confirm payer can produce both required signatures independently
Verify close() has no implicit payee protection (no check for gracePeriodEnd, no payee signature)
Test with settled=0 (full deposit theft) and settled>0 (partial theft)

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for one close() transaction (~0.001 USD on Tempo)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of channel deposit minus already-settled amount
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near 100% — attacker recovers their own deposit after consuming services

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After consuming maximum service with minimum settlement by payee
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; direct theft from payee

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Payee (service provider) of the target channel
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of all unsettled service earnings
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire unsettled deposit value across all channels where payer=authorizedSigner

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if any channels have authorizedSigner != payer (payee-protective configuration)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify delta=0 when cumulativeAmount=settled, full refund to payer

validation_result.mechanism: Missing payee signature in cooperative close allows payer unilateral finalization
validation_result.impact_summary: The payer can unilaterally call close() with cumulativeAmount equal to the current settled amount (or 0 if nothing is settled), bypassing the grace period and recovering the full remaining deposit. The payee loses all unsettled earnings for services already provided.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The close() function is labeled 'cooperative close' and is designed to bypass the grace period. However, it only verifies two signatures: (1) the voucher signature from channel.authorizedSigner, and (2) a CLOSE message signature from channel.payer. Since the payer sets authorizedSigner during openChannel() and typically sets it to themselves or a key they control, the payer controls BOTH required signatures. There is no payee signature requirement anywhere in close(). This means the payer can unilaterally finalize the channel at any time, choosing a cumulativeAmount equal to channel.settled (or 0 if nothing has been settled), thereby recovering the full remaining deposit and depriving the payee of any unsettled earnings. The grace period (1 hour) that exists in the initiateClose/finalize path to protect the payee is completely bypassed.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The close() function at TempoStreamChannel.sol verifies signer == channel.authorizedSigner and payerSigner == channel.payer, but never verifies any signature from channel.payee. The payer controls authorizedSigner (set in openChannel by the payer themselves). The function immediately sets channel.finalized = true and distributes funds based on the payer-chosen cumulativeAmount, with no grace period check.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function verifies signatures only from authorizedSigner and payer - both controlled by the payer. No payee signature or consent is required. The payer can set cumulativeAmount = channel.settled to pay the payee nothing for unsettled work, while recovering the full remaining deposit. This bypasses the grace period protection entirely.

The payer sets authorizedSigner to any address they choose. They can set it to themselves (msg.sender) or to another key they control. This gives them control over both signatures required by close().

calling_sequence: 1. Payer opens channel via openChannel() with authorizedSigner = payer's address (or controlled key)
2. Payee provides service off-chain, payer issues vouchers off-chain but payee hasn't settled on-chain yet (channel.settled = 0)
3. Payer creates a voucher with cumulativeAmount = 0 (or channel.settled), signs it with authorizedSigner key
4. Payer signs CLOSE message with payer key: keccak256('CLOSE', channelId, 0)
6. Signer recovery: signer == authorizedSigner ✓ (payer controls this key)
7. Payer recovery: payerSigner == channel.payer ✓ (payer signed this)
8. channel.finalized = true (immediate, no grace period check)
10. payerRefund = deposit - 0 = deposit, full refund to payer
11. Payee loses all unsettled service value
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The close() function checks signer != channel.authorizedSigner and payerSigner != channel.payer but has NO check for payee consent. Since the payer controls both authorizedSigner (set by payer in openChannel) and payer (is the payer), the function effectively allows unilateral closure. The grace period (checked in finalize via gracePeriodEnd) is entirely bypassed because close() never checks gracePeriodEnd. The payee's only protection was the grace period in the initiateClose→finalize path, which is made irrelevant by this cooperative close path.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The vulnerability exists in the contract code itself regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is fully valid and straightforward: (1) Payer opens channel with authorizedSigner = self. (2) Payee provides service, accumulates vouchers off-chain. (3) Before payee settles on-chain, payer calls close() with cumulativeAmount = 0 (or channel.settled). (4) Payer signs both required signatures (voucher and CLOSE message). (5) Channel is immediately finalized, payer gets full deposit back. Even if cumulativeAmount must be >= channel.settled (to avoid underflow), the payer can still set cumulativeAmount = channel.settled, paying zero for any unsettled work. The only constraint is Solidity 0.8's checked arithmetic: cumulativeAmount - channel.settled must not underflow, and channel.deposit - voucher.cumulativeAmount must not underflow. Both are satisfied when cumulativeAmount = channel.settled.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. This breaks the fundamental payment guarantee of the protocol. The payee (service provider) has no protection against a dishonest payer who consumes services and then immediately closes the channel without paying. The grace period mechanism is rendered useless since the cooperative close path bypasses it entirely without requiring payee consent. Any payer who controls their authorizedSigner key (the default and expected case) can steal all unsettled service value from any payee.
recommended_mitigations: Require the payee's signature in close() to make it truly cooperative. Add a third signature parameter verified against channel.payee.
Alternatively, require that the payee (not just authorizedSigner) signs the voucher used in close(), ensuring the payee agrees to the final cumulativeAmount.
As a minimum mitigation, enforce that cumulativeAmount in close() >= some minimum threshold or is validated against the latest settled nonce to prevent closing with stale/zero amounts.
Consider requiring that close() can only use vouchers with nonce > settledNonces[channelId] and check voucher.expiry, similar to settle().
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy TempoStreamChannel contract. 2. Deploy a mock ERC20 token. 3. Payer calls openChannel with authorizedSigner = payer, deposit = 1000 tokens. 4. (Off-chain) Payer issues vouchers to payee but payee does NOT call settle(). 5. Payer creates voucher with cumulativeAmount = 0, nonce = 1, expiry = far future. 6. Payer signs voucher with authorizedSigner key (EIP-712). 7. Payer signs CLOSE message with payer key. 8. Call close() with both signatures. 9. Verify channel is finalized, payee received 0 tokens, payer received full 1000 tokens back.
uncertainties: The design intent is unclear - it's possible the protocol designers intended the payer to control the authorizedSigner and assumed off-chain enforcement. However, on-chain the code provides no payee protection in the cooperative close path.

If there exist channels where authorizedSigner is controlled by the payee or a mutually trusted third party, those specific channels would not be vulnerable. But the default design (payer controls authorizedSigner) makes this exploitable.
additional_testing_needed: Test with channel.settled > 0 to confirm the payer can still close with cumulativeAmount = channel.settled (paying zero additional)
Test cross-interaction: payer calls initiateClose first, then immediately calls close() to bypass the grace period
Test with authorizedSigner != payer but controlled by payer (delegated hot wallet scenario)
Verify mempool visibility - can the payee front-run with a settle() call when they see a close() transaction?
